title: Swollen and Hot Testicles in Dogs: Symptoms, Causes, and Urgent Care
meta_desc: If your dog's testicles are hot and swollen, it may be a veterinary emergency. Learn about orchitis, torsion, and why you need to see a vet now.
content_html:
TL;DR: Swollen or hot testicles in a dog are a high-priority emergency that may indicate infection or life-threatening torsion, requiring immediate veterinary intervention.

What causes sudden testicular swelling or heat in my dog?

  • Noticeable swelling, firmness, or heat typically indicates serious conditions like orchitis (infection or inflammation), or epididymitis.
  • It can also be caused by testicular torsion, a rare but life-threatening twisting of the spermatic cord.
  • These conditions are extremely painful and can lead to permanent damage or systemic illness if the infection spreads to the bloodstream.

Is my dog's testicular swelling a veterinary emergency?

  • Yes, this is a high-priority emergency requiring immediate veterinary care.
  • Heat and rapid swelling in the scrotum are signs of acute inflammation or a critical loss of blood flow.
  • Professional exams are necessary to distinguish between a localized infection and a surgical emergency like torsion.
  • Delaying treatment can lead to tissue death (necrosis) and severe distress for your pet.

How does sharing a photo of my dog's condition help with triage?

  • A clear, well-lit photo allows the medical team to evaluate the degree of swelling, skin discoloration, or the presence of discharge.
  • Visual information helps telehealth providers or clinic staff significantly speed up the triage process.
  • Sharing a photo helps the veterinary team determine the level of urgency and ensures they are fully prepared to treat your dog the moment you arrive.

Clinical Context (Merck Veterinary Manual)

Acute inflammation of the testis (orchitis) or epididymis (epididymitis) in dogs can stem from trauma, infection (fungal, bacterial, or viral), or testicular torsion, with infection potentially arising from hematogenous or urologic sources. Clinical signs include pain and swelling of the testes, epididymides, and/or scrotum, sometimes accompanied by wounds or lesions on the scrotal skin. Palpation of the scrotal contents is essential to identify involved structures, although patient discomfort and edema can complicate this process. Ultrasonography, often with sedation or analgesia, is valuable for further evaluation, confirming testicular torsion, and detecting focal lesions such as masses or abscesses within the testis or epididymis; it can also reveal scrotal hernias or hematomas. Diagnostic testing should include evaluation for Brucella canis infection, and semen cytology with bacterial and mycoplasmal culture can be helpful, although collection may be difficult in ill or painful animals; fine-needle aspiration of the testis or epididymis can be performed for cytology and culture, with testicular biopsy reserved for cases where less invasive tests are insufficient.

Chapter: Urology, Infectious Disease, Surgery

Source: The Merck Veterinary Manual, 11th Edition (Page 1400)

faqs.question: Can I give my dog human pain medication for the swelling?
faqs.answer: No. Never give human medications like ibuprofen or acetaminophen to dogs, as they are toxic. Your veterinarian will provide safe, pet-specific pain relief.

faqs.question: What are other symptoms of testicular issues in dogs?
faqs.answer: Watch for lethargy, fever, loss of appetite, excessive licking of the area, or a 'hunched' walking gait due to abdominal pain.

faqs.question: Can neutered dogs have scrotal swelling?
faqs.answer: While rare, a recently neutered dog can develop a scrotal hematoma (a collection of blood). This also requires immediate veterinary attention to prevent infection.
